questions.title: Animal Moral Weights
questions.heading: How much do you value animal welfare relative to human welfare?
questions.info: Moral weights determine how much you value improvements to different types of beings. A weight of 1.0 for human life-years is the baseline. Lower weights for animals mean you think improving an animal's life is worth less than improving a human's life by the same amount.

options.label: Humans first
options.description: Heavily prioritize human welfare. Animal weights are very low.

options.label: Balanced
options.description: Give moderate weight to animal welfare alongside human welfare.

options.label: Animals matter a lot
options.description: Give significant weight to animal welfare. Animals can matter nearly as much as humans.

moreOptions.label: Only humans matter
moreOptions.description: Zero weight on all non-human animals.

moreOptions.label: Invertebrates count
moreOptions.description: Meaningful weight on shrimp and invertebrates alongside other animals.

questions.title: Time Discounting
questions.heading: How much do you discount effects that happen further in the future?
questions.info: Discount factors determine how much you value effects at different time horizons. A value of 100% means full weight; 0% means you ignore effects in that time period entirely. Near-term focused views discount the far future heavily; longtermist views keep high values across all periods.

options.label: No discounting
options.description: Value all time periods equally. The far future matters just as much as today.

options.label: Moderate discounting
options.description: Gradually reduce weight for more distant effects.

options.label: Near-term focus
options.description: Strongly prioritize effects in the next 10 years.

moreOptions.label: Longtermist
moreOptions.description: High value on all periods, especially the long run.

moreOptions.label: Medium-term focus
moreOptions.description: Prioritize the next 20 years, then drop off.

questions.title: Extinction Risk
questions.heading: How much should potential extinction risk discount non-x-risk projects?
questions.info: If you believe there's a significant chance humanity goes extinct, that reduces the expected long-term value of projects that don't address existential risks. A higher value here means you think extinction is more likely, which favors x-risk-reduction projects.

options.label: No extinction discount
options.description: Don't discount projects for extinction risk (0%).

questions.title: Risk Attitude
questions.heading: What is your attitude toward risk and uncertainty in cost-effectiveness estimates?
questions.info: Cost-effectiveness estimates come with significant uncertainty. 'Neutral' takes estimates at face value. Other options adjust for how you feel about upside or downside risk in these estimates.

options.label: Risk neutral
options.description: Take estimates at face value, no adjustment.

options.label: Upside skeptical
options.description: Discount projects with very high estimates — they might be too good to be true.

options.label: Downside focused
options.description: Weight negative outcomes more heavily — avoid projects that could cause harm.

options.label: Combined caution
options.description: Both skeptical of high upside and sensitive to downside risk.
